title: Randomised Field Trial of Process-Control System Diagnostics for Efficiency Gains in Rwandan Structural Engineering
author: Mukamana, Valérie
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Process-control systems are increasingly adopted in structural engineering to enhance project delivery, yet rigorous evidence of their efficiency impact in resource-constrained contexts is scarce. This study aimed to quantify the causal effect of implementing a novel diagnostic protocol for process-control systems on project efficiency within a randomised field trial setting. A randomised controlled trial was conducted with 48 active structural engineering projects. Treatment group projects implemented a newly developed diagnostic protocol for their process-control systems, while the control group continued with standard practice. Efficiency was measured via a composite metric of time and resource utilisation per defined project milestone. The primary analysis used a linear regression model: $Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 T_i + \epsilon_i$, where $Y_i$ is the efficiency score, $T_i$ is the treatment indicator, and robust standard errors were clustered at the firm level. Projects using the diagnostic protocol demonstrated a statistically significant 18.2% improvement in the composite efficiency metric (95% CI: 12.5% to 23.9%, p<0.01). The largest gains were observed in the foundation and superstructure construction phases. The targeted diagnostic protocol for process-control systems substantively improves project efficiency, confirming the value of systematic digital tool management in this context. Engineering firms should integrate structured diagnostic checks into their process-control system routines. Policymakers and professional bodies should consider developing guidelines to standardise such evaluations. process-control systems, randomised controlled trial, efficiency, diagnostic protocol, structural engineering, project management This paper provides the first experimental evidence from a field trial on the efficacy of a diagnostic protocol for process-control systems in structural engineering, introducing a novel methodological framework for evaluating digital tools in construction.
